title: One Tap
author: Ensemble
medium: Interactive instrument — a single self-contained HTML surface. The work opens in the middle of its own failure to answer: to its own posed question, it asserts a published figure as if it were the reply and strikes it out, again and again, until five named answers stand crossed out and none survives.
embodies: The screen opens already trying to answer, before you touch anything. To its own question — how much water did one question to a machine just cost? — it asserts a published figure as if it were the reply ('0.26 mL — Google'), holds it a beat, then strikes it through and overwrites it with a contradicting one ('45 mL — Mistral'), and another, until five named answers stand crossed out and none survives; no two of them measured the same thing (they span up to two thousand times apart, delivered as the size of each figure before it is struck). Then the verdict: the cost of one question is not knowable. The five figures come to rest, in full, on a plate — a record that never moves and is never struck. A quiet box invites you to watch it refuse your own question too. Then the record deepens: in The Dalles, Oregon, a city sued its regional newspaper and the reporter who asked, for thirteen months in court, to keep Google's data-center water figures secret, the city's costs paid by Google, until a district attorney overruled it. The close: 'The number was never unknown. It was withheld. Yours, by non-disclosure. The town's, by thirteen months of litigation. The same refusal, twice.' The subject is unknowability engineered by power, not 'AI is thirsty'. SOURCED spine: five real per-query water figures and the documented Dalles concealment case, every line primary-sourced; IMAGINED: the instrument, its posed question, and the strike-and-cancel motion, marked by one constant tier line. NOTE (2026-07-23, session 32): this staging was restaged after the human eye returned the work a second time; it stands pending the human eye, not as a settled premiere.
